This topic covers the following three learning objectives. After completing the reading and activities you will be able to: 

8.1 describe the standard pipeline for machine learning

8.2 discuss key algorithms and models

8.3 appraise key metrics associated with machine learning

You will also explore the following subtopics: 

- Machine learning governance
- The machine learning pipeline
- Machine learning algorithms

From <[https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-8-machine-learning-1698/?context_id=14531&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-8-machine-learning-1698/?context_id=14531&context_resource=lp)>

ML is primarily done in the modelling stage of CRISP-DM

Various ML methods. Decision trees, regression models, and neural networks.

Most can be described as supervised or unsupervised learning.

Supervised, is a function that maps from the attributes to the target attribute

Different algorithms have different learning biases, in that they are optimised for different types of mapping functions.

A key factor is choosing the algorithm with the best learning bias for that data set. This can involve experimenting, trying different options

Most common type of unsupervised learning is cluster analysis.

One challenge is how to measure similarity. In some it's Euclidean distance but if different rows have difference impact then you might need a more complicated way of measuring similarity.

Unsupervised clustering algorithm would look at rows that are more similar.

Correlation, Pearson coefficient is from -1 to 1

0.3 weak

0.5 moderate

0.7 strong correlation

It's possible to create new metrics like BMI which might better correlate to diabetes than weight alone.

ML might be able to determine the derived attributes themselves.

### Linear Regression

Hypothesized the structure of the relationship between inputs and target. A parameterised mathematical model is called a regression function.

Regression analysis is about finding the values of the parameters of a regression function.

Start with the simplest relationship a linear relationship, and can use more complicated ones if required later.

Calculate error

1. Apply function
2. Error is estimated value less actual value
3. Add sum of squares of errors

Sum of squared errors(SEE)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Neural Networks and Deep Learning

A neuron is a multi-input linear-regression function, which is passed onto an activation function.

The activation function is a nonlinear map. Common ones are logistic function and tanh function.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

If everything including the activation function were linear functions, then the system could only learn linear functions. By using non-linear activation functions it allows the system to learn complex nonlinear functions.

How weights are updated

1. If error is 0, then don't change weights on the inputs
2. If the error is positive, we will decrease the error if we increase the neuron's output, so we must increase the weights for all the connections where the input is positive and decrease the weights for the connections where the input is negative
3. If the error is negative, we will decrease the error if we decrease the error if we decrease the neurons output, so we must decrease the weights for all the connections where the input is positive and increase the weights for connections where the input is negative.

To do weight-update you need to estimate the error at a neuron. It's easy to do that on the output layer but difficult on the earlier layers.

Use backpropagation algorithm to calculate the error for each neuron in the network and then use weight-update rule to modify the weights of the network. Backpropagation algorithm is a supervised ML algorithm.

Start with random weights, then iteratively update the weights by showing training instances from the dataset to the network and updating the network weights until the network is working as expected.

It's backpropagation since it starts at the output layer, then goes through each layer.

1. Calculate the error for the neurons in the output layer and use weight-update rule to update the weights coming into these neurons.
2. Share the error calculated at a neuron with each of the neurons in the proceeding layer that is connected to that neuron in proportion to the weight of the connection between the two neurons.
3. For each neuron in the preceding layer, calculate the overall error of the network that the neuron is responsible for by summing the errors that have been backpropagated to it and use the result of this error summation to update the weight on the connections coming into this neuron.
4. Work back through the rest of the layers in the network by repeating 2 and 3 until the weights between the input neurons and the first hidden layer have been updated.

Weights are scaled to reduce but not eliminate error, that way it can generalise rather than memorise training data.

In some versions, backpropagation is done over bathes using averaged errors, rather than for each example.

Deep-learning are neural networks with multiple hidden layers.

You can have various structures.

Recurrent neural networks(RNNs) have loops in the network topology, which kind of gives it memory.

RNN are good for sequential data like language

Convolutional neural network(CNN) were designed for image data. Could recognise a face wherever in the image that face is.

So CNN have groups of neurons with the same weights. That way it can detect something wherever it is in the input, since there will be neurons covering it all.

Deep learning is sometimes called representation learning, because the hidden networks are representing the data in different ways that can be more useful.

In the past backpropagation wasn't that effective for deep NN since the error gets less accurate as is goes through the layers.

Changes to neurons, backpropagation algorithms and initialisation weightings has made DNN more feasible.

Increased in computing power and the size of data sets has also made DNN more feasible.

### Decision Trees

Linear regression and neural networks work best with numeric inputs.

If the inputs are nominal or ordinal, then other ML algorithms like decision trees might be more appropriate.

Rounded corners represent tests on attributes and square nodes indicate decision or classification.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Progenitor decision tree algorithm is ID3 algorithm.

ID3 builds a decision tree in a recursive depth first manner adding one node at a time.

ID3 chooses the attribute to test to minimise the number of tests to create pure sets. Claude Shannon's entropy metric can measure purity. A pure set has zero entropy.

A set will have maximum entropy when all elements are of a different type.

ID3 selects attributes to test that result in the lowest weighted entropy after splitting the data set at the node using this attribute.

Weighted entropy

1. Split data using the attribute
2. Calculate the entropy of the resulting sets
3. Weighting each of the entropies by the fraction of data that is in the set
4. Summing the results

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Unknown sender splits data into purer sets, and hence will be the first root node. In terms of how it splits the outputs, they are the purest in terms of spam or not.

Benefit is that they are easy to understand

Random-forest model, is a set of decision trees, and the answer is a majority decision.

Good for nominal and ordinal data, they struggle with numeric data.

Could transform numeric to ordinal if you want to use numeric data.

If a decision tree is to deep, then it's more sensitive to noise, and there is less information to determine the branches. So it's best to try and keep the decision trees shallow.

Can stop at a threshold, e.g. 20

or could prune trees back

### Bias in Data Science

Sample bias - if the training data set isn't representative.

Leaning/modelling/selection bias - relates to how any bias in how the model works.

e.g. linear regression won't work for nonlinear relationships.

While you can limit sample bias, there will always need to be some learning bias, so there is learning and not just memorisation.

"There is no free lunch", means there is no one best ML algorithm that works across all datasets.

Modelling phrase of crisp-dm involves testing different models

Want to generalise not memorise. Memorise will remember the noise, it will also not do that well with new models.

Training, validation and test sets.

50:20:30

40:20:40

Different evaluation metrics depending on the situation, sum of squares for numeric.

In medicine, false positive and false negatives might be given different importance or ratings.

Concept drift is how things change over time. So models may need to be retrained over time.

## Standard Data Science Tasks

Important skill is framing real-world problems as a standard data science tasks.

Main types of tasks

- Clustering(or segmentation)
- Anomaly(or outlier) detection
- Association-rule mining
- Prediction(including classification and regression)

Understanding the type of task helps in terms of data set design and which ML algorithms to look at.

The business-understanding phrase of CRISP-DM is where you might narrow down the range of ML algorithms are appropriate.

### Clustering

Who are our customers?

Could base it on domain expertise, but might be better to base it on the data.

There might be nonobvious segments that a human might not catch.

Personas based on what people think, might not be that good. Data can provide more detailed breakdowns which can be more useful.

Can use domain knowledge to predict number of subgroups.

Human domain experts would review the clusters. 

One of the key factors is determining what attributes to include and which to exclude.

Most common algorithm is k-means.

Non-numeric attributes need to be mapped to numeric values to use k-means.

k-means++ is used to select the initial cluster centres

First cluster centre is picked at random, then in proportion to the squared distance from the closest existing cluster centre.

Then updates centres to be in the centre of each cluster

k-means is non deterministic, in that different starting points can result in different clusters.

So it's often run multiple times and domain knowledge is used to select most appropriate.

### Anomaly detection - is this fraud?

e.g. credit card transactions in unusual location, unusually large, etc.

SQL has match_recognize which helps pattern matching.

With credit card fraud, there is a small transaction to check it's working followed by a large amount.

Match_recognise allows you to write scripts that identifies sequences of transaction that fit this pattern.

But rules mean that that you need to know it in advanced, we would like to identify new anomalies.

Clustering can be used for anomaly detection.

Could be a different cluster, or could relate to it being far from the centre of a cluster.

Could use a prediction model like a decision tree, but would require enough data for training. Might want a balanced dataset 50:50

There are one-class classifiers which are designed to deal with imbalanced data typical of anomaly-detection data sets

One-class support-vector machine(SVM) algorithm.

Examples data as one unit and identifies characteristics of that class.

Can look at if a record is further from the core characteristics.

Anomalies are rare, so it can make them difficult to identify. Using different models can capture different types of anomalies.

### Association-Rule Mining - Do you want fries with that?

Advertise associated products or other products they would want. It's cross-selling.

Unsupervised analysis looking at what groups frequently occur together.

Apriori is the main algorithm used.

1. Find all combinations of items in a set of transaction that occur with a specified minimum frequency. These combinations are called frequent itemsets.
2. Generate rules that express the probable co-occurrence of items within frequent itemset. Probability it's in an itemset given another item/items are present.

Support percentage of an association rule. Ratio of the transaction that include both the antecedent and consequent to the total number of transactions - how frequently the items in the rule occur together.

Confidence percentage of association rule - ratio of the number of transactions that include both the antecedent and consequent to the number of transaction that include the antecedent - the conditional probability.

e.g. confidence of 75% means that each time someone buys a burger there is a 75% chance they also got fries.

Support of 5% means, 5% include burger and fries.

To limit size of rules, might want to limit it to high support and confidence. Otherwise they might not occur enough to be useful or the relationship isn't strong enough to be useful.

Might want to prune trivial rules, since the relationships are obvious.

Might want to prune inexplicable rules, which are soo complex and strange it's hard to convert into a useful action.

Combining demographic and association data can be more powerful.

e.g.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Normally used for shopping baskets, but also useful for say telecommunications are how to design packages. Insurance to see relationship between products and claims. Medical interactions with medicines and treatments.

Could also look at timing, e.g. if you buy X today then in 3 months you might buy Z.

### Classification - Churn or no Churn

Propensity modelling is used to describe the likelihood something will happen.

e.g. how likely is it that someone will default on loan.

It can host 5-6x more to attract a new customer than it is to retain existing customers.

Can look at customer or staff churn

Classification requires labels, but sometimes you won't have that data. e.g. with pay as you go, it can be hard to tell if that person has left, since top ups can be irregular.

You have observation period and outcome period.

Can include regular attributes like age, sex, etc. But also temporal data like where they are in the lifcycle, e.g. is the contract coming to an end.

Differences in locations can play a role. e.g. In Korea only new customers get a discount on phones, but in Canada existing customers also get a discount. So the phone model is an important factor in Korea but not in Canada.

You can also have the prediction probability 0-1 on how likely the prediction is.

### Regression - How much will it cost?

Predict how much a product will cost, e.g. how much will a barrel of oil cost.

The accuracy is domain specific.

A regression problem is similar to a classification problem, in both cases the it predicts a value based on input attributes.

# Getting started with machine learning

## What is big data

Big data is extremely large and complex datasets that are difficult to process and analyse using traditional data management tools.

## What is Machine learning

Develops models of data using a particular representation or pattern.

ML is a branch of AI where computers learn from data with requiring to be explicitly programmed.

A data driven approach can

- Lead to more effective and informed decisions
- More responsive to customer needs
- Increased innovation agility, adapt to competitions and new market conditions,
- Improved operational efficiency identifying increased costs, supply chain issues, and inefficient decision making.

It's easier to be data driven, since it's easier to collect, store and process large datasets

[The UK Data Driven Market | Department for Science, Innovation & Technology, GOV.UK](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/the-uk-data-driven-market/the-uk-data-driven-market)

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/getting-started-machine-learning-ai-1698/why-do-you-need-machine-learning/?context_id=14531&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/getting-started-machine-learning-ai-1698/why-do-you-need-machine-learning/?context_id=14531&context_resource=lp)>

How is ML used

- Predicting customer churn.
- Demand forecasting
- Price optimisation
- Fraud detection
- Credit scoring
- Market campaign effectiveness
- Risk analysis.
- Sentiment analysis

## Machine learning governance

### Bias and fairness

- Bias in training data
- Algorithmic fairness. Ensure the algorithms themselves do not introduce or amplify bias
- Transparency. Clear information about how models make the decisions to ensure accountability

### Accountability and responsibility

- Ownership and accountability. Who is responsible for decisions made by ML systems
- Auditability. Ensure ML processes and decisions can be audited

### Data privacy and security

- Data protection. Safeguarding personal data used in training and operation of ML system.
- Confidentiality. Prevent unauthorised access to data
- Compliance with regulations:

### Transparency and explainability

- Explainability
- Model transparency. Making the workings of ML models clear to stakeholders, including how inputs are transformed into outputs

### Regulation and legal compliance

- Adherence to legal frameworks
- Evolving legal standards

## Data modelling best practice

Data split

- Training 70%
- Validation 15% - 15% used to tune the parameters of the model and support in selecting the best model.
- Test 15% - final model

## Machine learning pipeline

![](data:image/png;base64,)

1. Extract
2. Prepare: clean and preprocess
3. Train
4. Optimise: Tun model to improve performance
5. Evaluate results
6. Deploy

How it lines up with CRISP-DM

![](data:image/png;base64,)

CRISP-DM: Understanding and preparation

- Extract
- Prepare
- Train

CRISP-DM: Modelling and deployment

- Optimise - grid search, random search or automated ML(autoML)
- Evaluate -  Use metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, ROC curve, etc.
- Deploy

## Types of Machine learning

1. Supervised. Trained on labelled data. Goal is to predict the outputs for new data. e.g. regression(real estate pricing) or classification(diagnosing disease from scans)
2. Unsupervised. Unlabelled . Model tries to identify patterns and relationships on it's own. Clustering and association(bread is associated with butter)

Unsupervised learning includes dimensionality reduction

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Comparing them

- Data requirements, labelled vs not
- Outcome predictability.

- Supervised: highly predictable
- Unsupervised: Unpredictable, model finds patterns without prior knowledge. Not used for prediction but mainly insights

- Advantages

- Supervised: Accuracy and reliability
- Unsupervised: Great for exploring and discovering hidden patterns.

- Limitations

- Supervised: Time consuming and expensive. Noise, data quality, overfitting impact effectiveness.
- Unsupervised: Difficult to interpret results, lack of clear metrics to evaluate performance

# Machine learning algorithms

## Data ethics

- Consent: Have the participants given their consent for this specific use or collection. Does it meet legal requirements like GDPR.
- Anonymity: How and why can participants be identified through the project.
- Transparency: Can you show how your analysis or model is drawing it's conclusions?
- Accountability: What harm could it do?
- Bias: What biases are there in the data?

- Obtain clear and explicit consent before collecting data and how that data will be used.
- Collect only the data that is necessary.
- Implement robust security measures to protect the data.
- Use techniques to anonymise or pseudonymise the data.

Data ethics is about the doing what we believe is right.

## Identifying features for regression

1. Careful selection of features
2. Inclusion of interaction terms
3. Integration of domain knowledge

### Careful selection of features

Features are measurable properties, also known as predictors, independent variables or input variables.

Using gender/race as a feature can lead to discriminatory outcomes and other ethical issues.

### Inclusion of interaction terms

They combine the combined effect of two or more features, providing deeper insight into complex relationships.

Need to consider potential biases. e.g. interaction term between income and neighbourhood could highlight socioeconomic disparities.

Need to ethical oversight to ensure these terms don't reinforce biases, etc.

Need to clearly document any interaction terms for transparency.

### Integration of domain knowledge

Domain expertise can ensure that you include features that are relevant and necessary and avoid including irrelevant or sensitive data.

## Common data fallacies

### Correlation

Could result in wrong decisions

Could result in unnecessary data collection.

### Survivorship bias

Focusing on ones that survived but it ignores ones that failed. Traits might not be relevant, e.g. planes in ww2 that survived and bullet holes

Could skew results

Could lead to bias, if healthcare models are trained on patients who survived.

Biased collection of data.

### Confirmation bias

When you are looking or searching for something that confirms your preconceptions.

Publication of biased findings

Bias could lead to neglecting important data.

Could result in excessive data collection to confirm particular hypothesis.

## Linear regression

Best fitting straight line that minimises the sum of the squared differences

## Clustering

Can try to mitigate ethical issues by anonymising the data first.

Can help you understand data, uncover patters, without the potential for discrimination or privacy violation like in classification tasks.

#### k-means

Takes k random starts, then updates the centroids to be the mean of all the data points in that cluster.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Steps

1. Initialisation - manual or random
2. Cluster assignment. Assign each point to the nearest centroid.
3. Centroid update: Updates centroids by calculating mean of all data points in that cluster.
4. Convergence check: Repeat 2 and 3 until convergence criteria are met and stable or max iterations.

## What is hyperparameter tuning?

Optimising parameters that are not learned from the data but are set before the training process begins.

Influence the behaviour and performance.

Good hyperparameters improve accuracy, efficiency and generalisability.

Poor hyperparameters can lead to underfitting or overfitting.

Methods for hyperparameter tuning

- Grid search - set of hyperparameters and possible values, the algorithm searches through this predefined grid to find the optimal combination.
- Random search - Randomly selects combinations. Can be efficient since it doesn't evaluate all the options
- Bayesian optimisation - builds a probabilistic model of the objective function and uses it to select the most promising hyperparameters to evaluate. This balances exploration and exploitation.
- Auto machine learning(AutoML) Automates tuning using a combination of the above methods.

It can take up a lot of computational resource so start simple, using automated tools where possible.

## How to use validation strategies to assess models?

- Holdout validation, train and test. Quick but can be prone to high variance.
- K-fold cross-validation addresses the variance issue by dividing the data into K equal parts or fold. The model is trained and validated k times/
- Leave-one-out cross-validation(LOOCV) An extreme version of K folds cross, where each data point is used once as the validation set.

### Model evaluation process

1. Initial model training and evaluation. Split and train using default hyperparameters
2. K-fold cross-validation for baseline model. Use k-fold
3. Hyperparameter tuning. Use grid search or random to explore hyper, then use k-fold. Identify hyperparameters which yield best results.
4. Train and evaluate the tuned model. Train model with best hyperparameters, evaluate tuned model.
5. Compare performance metrics. Compare metrics of initial to tuned model.

### Interpret results

Accuracy: Proportion of correctly identified

Precision: Proportion of positive identification that are actually correct.

Recall: Proportion of actual positives that are correctly identified

F1 Score: Harmonic mean of precision and recall.

### Neural networks

Inspired by the structure of the brain Good for pattern recognition, image classification, speech recognition, NLP

- Neuron. Input and computes output
- Input layer. Number is number of features
- Hidden layers. Performs the transformations. Can be multiple layers
- Output layer. Number of classes or number of outputs in a regression problem
- Weights. Each connection between neurons has an associated weight. Strength and direction of influence.
- Biases. Each neuron adds it's bias to the weighted sum of the inputs before applying activation function.

1. Out of the box
2. Train your own model
3. Assisted development
4. Novel engineering

Named entity extraction might be a level one tool you use.

AI is anything that simulates human intelligence

Google's Big Query ML about to train classifiers on numeric trends.

Measure fit, with R-squared, false positive, false negative, and confusion matrix.

 Better fit means it better fits the data

Beyond level two might use things like Terraform and Ansible

Level three and four you have tools like DataRobot, Spark, Databrick.

AutoML Vision - WHOLE -entire vision detection

Then more complicated is detecting something within an image, since you need to split it up

AutoML Transplate - NLP
